hallucinations.description: The paper repeatedly frames the comparison as RL-aligned versus DPO-aligned, but the code and the paper's own model description do not use a DPO-trained model. The second model is CodeLlama-7B-Instruct, which is only instruction-tuned in this setup. Thus the headline claim about 'DPO-aligned' models is unsupported by the actual experiment.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper title: "Error Type Distributions Differ Between RL-Aligned and DPO-Aligned Code Generation Models". Paper §3.1: "CodeLlama-7B-Instruct ... It is a general instruction-following model, not specifically trained with code-focused DPO." Code config: dpo_model_id = "codellama/CodeLlama-7b-Instruct-hf"

hallucinations.description: The paper claims to compare 'alignment methods' and to test RL versus DPO, but the executed experiment code labels the non-RL condition as 'dpo' purely as a variable name while loading a non-DPO model. This misreports the technical setup as a controlled alignment comparison when it is actually a comparison between two very different pretrained/fine-tuned models.
hallucinations.evidence: Code run_experiment.py / config.py: rl_model_id = "Salesforce/codet5-large-ntp-py" and dpo_model_id = "codellama/CodeLlama-7b-Instruct-hf". Code comments also say "Load codellama/CodeLlama-7b-Instruct-hf" while variables are named dpo_model / dpo_results.

hallucinations.description: The paper reports a fine-grained 19-category LlmFix analysis as if it were part of the described experiment, but the main H-E1 experiment code only computes a 3-category contingency table and does not perform any fine-grained taxonomy analysis. The fine-grained results therefore are not supported by the code associated with the main experiment described in the paper.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §5.5 reports: "Fine-grained level (9 observed categories of 19 possible): Chi-square = 525.40, df = 8 ... Cramér's V = 0.823" with a detailed 9-category table. But H-E1 analyze.py defines ERROR_CATEGORIES = ["syntax", "runtime", "assertion"] and build_contingency_table returns only a 2x3 table.

hallucinations.description: The paper reports execution-depth statistics, Welch's t-test, Cohen's d, confidence intervals, and trace success rates, but the main experiment code shown for H-E1 contains no execution-depth tracing or t-test computation. These reported results are therefore unsupported by the code for the paper's stated experiment.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §5.4 gives: "Welch's t-test: t = 14.47, p = 1.08 × 10⁻³⁴; Cohen's d = 1.69 ... 95% Confidence intervals ... Trace success rate was 100% for both models." In contrast, H-E1 code only includes chi-square analysis in analyze.py and no sys.settrace-based measurement or t-test.

hallucinations.description: The citation to an 'ICSE 2025 study by Wang et al. (2025)' on understanding code generation failures appears dubious and is not supported by any code or repository artifact. Given the future venue/year and generic title, this is likely an invented or unverifiable reference.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper §2.3: "The ICSE 2025 study by Wang et al. (2025) provides a three-tier classification based on execution semantics." References: "Wang, Y., Chen, B., Zhang, J., & Liu, X. (2025). Understanding Code Generation Failures: A Systematic Analysis of LLM-Generated Code Errors. ICSE."

hallucinations.description: The citation to 'LlmFix' with the given title and arXiv identifier appears likely fabricated or at least unverifiable from the provided materials. It is used as the basis for the 19-category taxonomy, but no external validation is provided and the taxonomy in code appears hand-authored rather than imported from an existing package or paper artifact.
hallucinations.evidence: References: "Zhang, W., Li, M., Wang, T., & Chen, H. (2024). LlmFix: A Large Language Model-Based Code Repair Framework with Fine-Grained Error Taxonomy. arXiv:2409.00676." H-M3 config manually hardcodes the taxonomy: LLMFIX_TAXONOMY = {...}

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issue is methodological misrepresentation: the paper is framed as RL vs DPO, but the compared non-RL model is not DPO-trained. In addition, major reported results on execution depth and fine-grained taxonomy are not supported by the main experiment code provided, making the paper's empirical claims substantially unreliable.
